rag_summary.**HEA alloy Ni29.988Cr15.9936Co9.996Al23: The oxidation performance of high-entropy alloys (HEAs) is critically governed by composition, processing-induced microstructure, and the resulting oxide scale characteristics. Compositions with high Al content (e.g., Al₂₃.₀₈-Co₁₅.₃₈-Cr₁₅.₃₈-Fe₁₅.₃₈-Ni₃₀.₇₈) and optimized BCC/B2 phase fractions (83.5%) achieve superior oxidation resistance due to the formation of a continuous, thin Al₂O₃ oxide layer (~9 μm). This protective scale limits further thickening, prevents oxygen ingress, and minimizes weight change (0.36 mg/cm²) and oxidation rate constants (Kₚ = 5.5×10⁻⁹ g²cm⁻⁴s⁻¹) by adhering during thermal cycling. The high BCC/B2 fraction is enabled by processing: arc melting with flipping ensures homogeneous Al distribution, while water quenching after 1200°C heat treatment preserves high-temperature Al redistribution, facilitating continuous Al₂O₃ formation. Conversely, alloys with lower Al (e.g., Al₁₅.₃₇₅-Co₁₅.₃₇₅-Cr₁₅.₃₇₅-Fe₁₅.₃₇₅-Ni₃₈.₅) or high Cr (e.g., Al₁₅.₃₈-Co₁₅.₃₈-Cr₂₃.₀₈-Fe₁₅.₃₈-Ni₃₀.₇₈) exhibit poorer performance. High Cr promotes Cr₂O₃-rich scales (16 μm), which spall due to CTE mismatch and volatilization, causing mass loss (0.29 mg/cm²) and higher oxidation rates (Kₚ ~1.5×10⁻⁹ g²cm⁻⁴s⁻¹). FCC-dominated structures (e.g., Al₁₆.₆₇-Co₁₆.₆₇-Cr₁₆.₆₇-Fe₁₆.₆₇-Ni₃₃.₃₂) form mixed Al₂O₃/Cr₂O₃/spinel oxides, leading to spallation and reduced Al₂O₃ continuity, resulting in higher oxidation rates (~10⁻⁹ g²cm⁻⁴s⁻¹) and weight changes (-0.07 mg/cm²). Doping with reactive elements (Y/Hf) significantly enhances oxidation resistance. For instance, Al₂₂.₄₃-Co₂₀.₃₉-Cr₁₆.₃₁-Fe₂₀.₃₉-Hf₀.₀₅-Ni₂₀.₃₉-Y₀.₀₅ achieves ultra-low oxidation rates (lower than FeCrAlYHf) and no spallation due to a coherent dual-phase nanostructure (A2/B2) that stabilizes uniform Y/Hf distribution. This suppresses sulfur segregation at the scale/metal interface, minimizes interfacial pores, and promotes columnar Al₂O₃ grains via inward oxygen diffusion, reducing growth stress (~-0.1 GPa) and residual stress (~-5.8 GPa). Ultra-fine lamellar eutectic structures (γ'+β, <500 nm) in Y/Hf-doped alloys (e.g., Al₂₀.₁₇-Co₁₉.₁₂-Cr₂₀.₂-Fe₂₀.₁₈-Hf₀.₀₁-Ni₂₀.₂₉-Y₀.₀₂) further improve interfacial toughness and reduce oxidation rates by 45-34% versus conventional alloys, as the fine structure enables homogeneous RE distribution and resists coarsening. Spinodally decomposed A2/B2 microstructures (e.g., Al₂₄.₅-Co₁₈.₉-Cr₁₈.₉-Fe₁₈.₉-Hf₀.₀₅-Ni₁₈.₉-Y₀.₀₅) form protective (Al,Cr)₂O₃ scales with minimal spallation and shallow internal attack (<20 μm) due to coherent cube-on-cube interfaces blocking corrosive species diffusion and high B2 phase fractions sustaining Al supply.
